headlines.title: WAREHOUSE LEVY FOUGHT
headlines.subtitle: Lattimore and Bailey Say Proposed Local Tax Is Unconstitutional. WILL MEET MONDAY Senate Cancels Bank Commissioner's Power to Appoint Managers.
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram, AUSTIN, Sept. 3 .- The senate consumed the entire forenoon discussing the warehouse bill which is being passed. It only reached section 4 when adjournment was taken until Monday morning at 10 o'clock. Senator Wiley obtained the adoption of an amendment to the bill taking away the power of the commissioner of insurance and banking to appoint local managers. He also had adopted an amendment striking out the assistance of state bank examiners to inaugurate the system. Senators Lattimore and Bailey of De Witt for two hours discussed the constitutionality of section 4, which allows cities and towns to levy a tax. Both maintained that this feature of the bill is unconstitutional, while Senator Collins insisted there is nothing in the constitution which prohibits cities and towns levying such a tax. Neither branch of the legislature will meet tomorrow. The house transacted practically no business this afternoon, the session lasting only a few minutes. The house adjourned until 4 o'clock Monday evening. Next Wednesday the governor expects to submit the question of raising the legal reserve of state banks to that required of national banks.

headlines.title: First Degree Team Sisters of Sons of Hermann Organized in Ft. Worth
headlines.fullText: The degree team of Thusnelda Lodge, Sisters of Sons of Herman, is the first organized in Texas or elsewhere. It is believed that the grand lodge which meets here next April will adopt its formula for the Initiation work of the order. In the picture are: Top row, left to right, Mrs. H. Wuchte, Miss Nellie Rosenbach, Miss Edna Schilder, Mrs. W. Walton, Mrs. A. Schlicher, Miss Frida Frank, Mrs. Emil Luzni and Mrs. Paul Schubert. Middle row, Miss Pauline Allmendinger, Mrs. A. Demalade, Miss Minnie Frank, Mrs. Carl Schilder, Mrs. F. Runge, Miss Ella Frank, Mrs. Wirtz, Miss Rosa Bothe, Miss Ruby Schilder and Miss Adella Mueller. Bottom row, Mrs. B. Bryan, Miss Matty Schilder, Mrs. F. Runge, Miss Ella Frank, Mrs. Wirtz, Miss Rosa Bothe, Miss Ruby Schilder and Miss Adella Mueller.

headlines.title: JAPS WILL BUY 2,000,000 BALES IN U. S. MARKET
headlines.subtitle: Eastern Spinners to Purchase More Than One Season's Supply of Staple.
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram. NEW YORK, Sept. 5 .- So-called "inside information" was given out in the cotton trade today to the effect that Japan will take from this season cotton crop nearly 2,000,000 bales. Criticism went so far as to say that Japan did not have spindles enough to handle 2,000,000 bales of cotton from the entire world. The fact is that Japan last year took nearly 500,000 bales of cotton from America, more than 1,000,000 from East India and 200,000 bales from Egypt. In estimating Japan's requirements from this year's cotton crop at 2,000,000 bales, or nearly all from America, it must be noted that Japan is getting on hand in the belief that cotton will at no time be cheaper than it is at present when finances all over the world are disorganized. The question is asked: "How are the Japs going to pay for it?" The answer is that sight bills in yen are being accumulated in New York. They are cleared through London to a more or less extent. In paying for $100,000,000 cotton through London the bills are rapidly reducing our debts abroad, enabling us in part to meet city and corporation obligations in London, and insuring a favorable balance of exchange toward New York much earlier than would have been the case had Japan not entered our cotton buying market. Japan is after the vast Chinese market for cotton piece goods and the crippling of Germany and Austria by the war offers Japan an opportunity for trade which she is quick to grasp and which has never been presented before. The destination of most of the thousands of cotton bales now passing through Texas is the warehouse as the first step in financing a crop for which there is no market. How long it will stay there no one can tell. A leading banker of this city, who is closely identified with the efforts to save the crop from slaughter, said: "At present we have no market. Active gathering of the cotton crop of this county alone requires $1,500,000, and for Texas it takes $40,000,000. In short, it costs $10 per bale to pick cotton. There is a general move now to erect warehouses in which to store it. When so stored it is believed that the regional banks, provided it becomes necessary, will advance money on it."

headlines.title: TAX IN 1915 ON COTTON LAND PLAN
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram. AUSTIN, Sept. 5 .-- Governor Colquitt this afternoon made public a letter from Senator Hoke Smith addressed to the currency and cotton situation. Amendments to the currency law have been passed and others will be passed. The difficulty of the situation is the lack of demand for a crop, thus creating a fear as to price, coupled with a fear of the size of the crop next year. "Suggestion has been made that the twelve Southern States buy 3,000,000 bales prorated among them according to their production for the past five years and issue notes due on or before five years. It is claimed that these notes would be accepted everywhere. The suggestion further carries the plan of a 50 cents per acre tax on all land planted in cotton to meet the interest on these notes and a further tax of not less than $10 an acre on any land planted in cotton in excess of one-half of the amount planted this year. The claim is made that the situation can be met by the certainty that next year's crop will be reduced."

headlines.title: STARTLING FACTS AT SMUGGLING INQUIRY
headlines.subtitle: Government Officials May Be Involved in Supposed Conspiracy.
headlines.fullText: SAN FRANCISCO, Sept. 5 .-- The trial of what is believed to be an organized body of Chinese smugglers was turned on in Houston several months ago and the investigation which closed here today. It is declared that revelations made here proved that the smugglers operate through this port as freely as they do through any port in the United States. No details of testimony adduced were made public and all of it will be embodied in a report to be made to Washington officials in a few weeks. Among the witnesses heard was D. S. Federal secret service. He occupied the witness stand for several days.

headlines.title: COP SUSPICIOUS ON CAR; TWO ARRESTED
headlines.subtitle: Two Dozen Bottles of Whisky Found in Suit Case-Bearer and Saloon Porter Accused.
headlines.fullText: Patrolman Newburg, riding a South Main car home to breakfast at 6:30 a. m. Saturday, thought there was something suspicious about Roland Briscoe, a negro living at 1210 Arizona avenue, carrying a suit case. Roland seemed to be having a hard time and the trip seemed to be heavy. "What you got in that grip?" asked the patrolman. "Clothes," said Roland. "Lemme see," said the officer, and he opened the grip. Instead of clothes he found two dozen bottles of whisky. Later Robert Norris, a porter at a Main street bar, was arrested. Complaints were filed against Norris, charging him with the theft of the whisky, and one against Briscoe, charging him with concealing stolen property.

headlines.title: TEACHERS APPROVE ENFORCED EDUCATION
headlines.subtitle: County Institute Wants State Legislature to Enact a Compulsory Law.
headlines.fullText: Before closing Saturday, the Tarrant County Teachers' Institute went on record as favoring compulsory education. A committee was appointed to carry a resolution, demanding that compulsory education be written on the state laws, to Tarrant county legislators. More than 200 teachers attended the institute. They will return to their respective school districts next week to get ready for the opening of the fall terms. The institute has been more beneficial than any other ever held, according to declarations by the officers in charge. Before adjourning the institute voted a resolution of thanks to E. E. Lockhart and others who had appeared before the assembly.

headlines.title: BIG CROWD EXPECTED TO HEAR BAILEY
headlines.fullText: Special to The Star-Telegram. GAINESVILLE, Texas, Sept. 5. This city will be the scene next Sunday of a large gathering to hear former Senator Bailey's address. Large delegations are expected from Denton, Dallas, Fort Worth, Cleburne, Wichita Falls and other places. Special rates have been secured on the railroads for 100 miles in each direction. A half dozen bands have been engaged to furnish music. At least 10,000 people of Cooke county are expected to be present to hear Mr. Bailey's speech, and on a conservative estimate 5,000 people from other places will be here.

headlines.title: 6,000 Jap Students Held.
headlines.fullText: LONDON, Sept. 5. -- A dispatch to the Reuter Telegram company from Amsterdam says information has reached here that 6,000 Japanese students from German universities have been captured on the Dutch-German frontier and imprisoned.

headlines.title: HURT IN FIGHT WITH CONDUCTOR, HE SAYS
headlines.subtitle: George Sykes Taken to Hospital With Gash in Forehead-Says He Was Joking.
headlines.fullText: George Sykes, 53, an employe of the Texas Rolling Mills, went to the City-County Hospital Saturday night in an ambulance and had four stitches taken in his forehead after a fight with a conductor of a car. He said the fight occurred at the end of the car line. Sykes said he made some joking remark as he got off the car. He had walked thirty or forty feet, he said, when the conductor came up behind him and slammed him in the head with his transfer punch. Sloan's ambulance took Sykes to the hospital, where Dr. Littlepage had dressed his wounds.

headlines.title: BOND SIGNERS TO SETTLE; AVOID SUIT
headlines.subtitle: County Auditor's Office Crowded With Persons Who Went on Convict Bail.
headlines.fullText: The letters sent out during the past week by County Auditor J. A. Mulkey to persons who went on convict bonds notifying them that suit would be started against them within ten days, if they did not settle, have been productive. The auditor's office the last few days has been thronged with persons who were anxious to avoid suits. Altogether 235 letters were sent out.

headlines.title: CASES ASSIGNED
headlines.subtitle: Judge Swayne Will Open Term on Oct. 5.
headlines.fullText: Judge James W. Swayne of the Seventeenth district court Saturday announced the assignment for the October term. The first week, beginning Oct. 5, will be given over to non-jury cases. The second week, beginning Oct. 12, will be devoted to the civil jury docket, followed by one more week, beginning Dec. 21, for non-jury cases.

headlines.title: Friends Celebrate His Birthday.
headlines.fullText: Sidney Whitney, Jr., celebrated his sixth birthday with a party Friday afternoon at the home of his parents, Mr. and Mrs. Sidney Whitney, 1209 West Magnolia avenue. Games were played during the afternoon and refreshments were served to the following: Lois and Kivi Jenkins, Maurine and Ruth Steward, Pauline and Mary Burtrand, Gertie and Luis McKnight, Stella Whitney, Alfred, Evelyn and Otis Arsemeaux, Ouray McCorstin, George Pickard, and Mary Rob Whitley.

headlines.title: MANY SHIPS TO U. S.
headlines.subtitle: Fruit Company Will Put Twenty-Five Under American Flag.
headlines.fullText: The United Fruit Company will unite under United States registry twenty-five steamers now engaged in trade with the West Indies and Central and South America, according to a statement issued at the company's office. Some ships under construction in England also will be given American registry.

headlines.title: YVES NAT CONCERT AROUSES INTEREST
headlines.subtitle: Newspapers Give Liberal Praise to Pianist-Expected in Fort Worth Soon.
headlines.fullText: Unusual interest is shown in the coming concert of Yves Nat, the famous French pianist, expected here soon. The Philadelphia Press says: "He is admirable as a Chopin interpreter. The audience accorded him an ovation." "Yves Nat is a phenomenally gifted young pianist," says the Milwaukee Free Press. "He displayed an astonishing variety of pianistic touch and tone, and is the master of his instrument." "Vincent d'Indy's playing by Yves Nat was a feature of Tetrazzini's numbers. He is a master musician," is the comment made by the Chicago Daily News. The Muskogee Daily Phoenix says: "His playing was a feature of Tetrazzini's numbers. He is a master musician."

headlines.title: COP HAS FAST CYCLE
headlines.subtitle: B. Busby, Speed Cop, Has Machine That Will Make Ninety Miles.
headlines.fullText: The fastest motorcycle ridden by any man in commission will be put into service Monday by the local department, with B. Busby in the saddle. Busby was reappointed to the force Saturday by Chief Montgomery. His machine, a specially built racer with a speed capacity of ninety miles per hour, was to have been out of the express office Saturday afternoon, but was delayed. Busby will be detailed to watch for speeders.

headlines.title: AUTO CLUB ELECTION COMES TUESDAY NIGHT
headlines.subtitle: Active Movement for Loop Drive to Lake Worth Probably Will Be Started.
headlines.fullText: Directors of the Fort Worth Automobile Club will meet in the basement of the Chamber of Commerce Tuesday at 8 p. m. to elect a president, two vice presidents and a secretary and treasurer. The directors were elected at the annual meeting of the club recently. They will probably start a movement for a loop drive to Lake Worth at the Tuesday night meeting.

headlines.title: RIVERSIDE PASTOR GOES TO PLAINVIEW
headlines.fullText: Rev. R. A. Highsmith has resigned his pastorate at the Riverside Christian Church and has accepted a call from the First Christian Church at Plainview. His resignation took effect two weeks ago. The Riverside church has not yet chosen a successor to Rev. Mr. Highsmith, and has no regular pastor. Highsmith was a 1914 graduate of Texas Christian University, and served as pastor of the church during a part of his senior year at the university. W. B. Higgins, a student in Brite College at T. C. U., is holding a revival at the church. The meetings have been successful and largely attended and the pulpit will be occupied during the remainder of the summer by members of the faculty of T. C. U. until a regular pastor is selected by the congregation.

headlines.title: Papal Reception Announced.
headlines.fullText: ROME, Sept. 5. -- According to an announcement made here today, Pope Benedict XV will hold a reception for the diplomatic envoys on Sept. 7.

headlines.title: WEATHERFORD
headlines.fullText: WEATHERFORD, Sept. 5 .-- Mrs. B. F. Lanham of New York, has been visiting Mrs. R. H. Foat. Mrs. Fred Egelhoff and daughter, Miss Avery, have returned from a three weeks' stay in Ballade. Mrs. W. F. Clark and little son, Bill, returned to Kansas City accompanied by Miss Little Ferguson. Charles Corn, Barney Holand, and Miss Ellen Powie have returned from Stamford, Bowie, Spur and other points. Mrs. W. H. Seymour and children have returned from a trip to Fort Worth. Col. and Mrs. G. M. Bowie have returned from a sojourn of about four weeks in Colorado and Wyoming. Mr. and Mrs. C. O. Louck of Fort Worth have been visiting the family of C. W. Annie McGlinchey of Aledo is spending the week-end with Miss Mary Know McKinsey. Miss Nye of Chillicothe, who has been visiting the Misses Webster, left for San Antonio. Mr. and Mrs. W. O. DeWees have returned after nearly three months in Yellowstone Park. They were accompanied by Miss Augusta Roberts, who spent the summer there with them. Str. and Mrs. C. C. Irvine of Fort Worth came in to visit her brother, W. A. Fuller of Mangum, Okla. H. La Leach of Alvord, Wise county, is visiting his brother, Dr. H. F. Leach. Misses Marjorie and Louise Edwards have returned from a visit to Batesville, Ark. Miss M. L. Jones of Loving is visiting Dr. I. P. Lanier. Myrtice Heifrin and children have returned from Colorado, where they spent the summer. J. T. Fielder of Arlington is visiting Mr. J. R. Price. Miss Dick Orton of Godley is visiting her sister, Mrs. Henry Means. Mrs. Elmer Hill has been spending a few days in Fort Worth. Str. and Mrs. B. C. Rhome of Fort Worth are visiting her parents, Mr. and Mrs. B. M. Rider, and enjoying the services. Mrs. Hessill from Hillsboro and Dallas, where she visited for several days, has returned to Worth after a visit to the family of Mrs. A. Irvine.

advertisements.description: Ready-to-Wear and Millinery Store. WALK UPSTAIRS AND SAVE A FEW DOLLARS. This is a brand-new place, a brand-new stock and if you will pay this new store a visit Tuesday morning, if you can, or any day this week that is convenient for you, you will see for yourself what a nice place it is and what really attractive prices you can obtain on that new fall hat or gown.

I beg to announce that I will be a candidate for re-election for second term as Water Commissioner (Place 4). In asking for a second term I refer the citizens to my record as commissioner. I have filled the office conscientiously. I would like to make a great many improvements and extensions in water and sewer mains of the city, but have confined myself to necessities. I have tried to treat every citizen as a public servant and at no time have I failed to give courteous treatment. I have retained employes on their merits and at no time have I dismissed an employe for political reasons. I have guarded the finances of this Department as conscientiously as I would my own. I have rehabilitated the Powell plant. I have installed the Holly and bought the Alberger pumps at the main plant. The fire department has not lacked water pressure since I have been in office. I have used filtered water when it was impossible to furnish Artesian Water, but have timed the using of filtered water as much as I could so that it would not inconvenience the public any more than could be helped (filtering at night), but as the consumption is irregular, this could not be done at all times. The Auditor's report shows a saving in the operating expenses in the first year of my administration of about $114,000. I solicit a close investigation of my record, and if you approve of my ability in administrating your Water Department, and my acts as a commissioner, I will appreciate your support, not only your vote on November 3rd, but your indorsement of my administration to your friends and acquaintances. L. H. Blanke (Advertisement)

events.eventName: Fort Worth Automobile Club Directors Meeting
events.venue: basement of the Chamber of Commerce
